coredata.dc:title: Color change mechanism and application of oxidized carbon dots/I2O4 composite (OCDs/I2O4) in alcoholic beverages
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Ming-yu
Li, Ao
Ji, Tian-hao
Jv, Da-jian
Xu, Zhe-mi
coredata.dc:description: Currently, ethanol detection in alcoholic beverages typically relies on complicated and costly approaches, including gas chromatography, liquid chromatography, and mass spectrometry, etc. Herein, we developed a non-toxicity, practical, low-cost and portable visual indicator for ethanol detection based on OCDs/I2O4 nanopowders. By color change, the OCDs/I2O4 can visually indicate ethanol concentration ranging from 14 % vol to 56 % vol and the detection limit can be further extended to 2 % vol with a UV-visible spectrum. The OCDs/I2O4 indicator shows good stability and long-term endurance for at least 18 months, and it can be used under different temperatures (at least from 3 °C to 40 °C) and humidity (at least from 10 % to 90 %). A possible mechanism for the color change has been proposed based on the covalent-link hydrogen bonding change between OCDs and I2O4. The proposed OCDs/I2O4 composite offers a convenient and practical solution for ethanol monitoring in various alcoholic beverages.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ethanol detection
Oxidized carbon dots
I2O4

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CDs, OCDs/I2O4, and OCDs
Color change mechanism of OCDs/I2O4 in EtOH

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Funding: This research was supported by the funding of the School of Light Industry Science and Engineering in Beijing Technology and Business University. We thank Shule Zhang for the detailed experiment suggestions and valuable discussion.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the preparation process of OCDs/I2O4 and the color change of OCDs/I2O4 in various liquors.
The XRD patterns of CDs, OCDs/I2O4, and OCDs (a); the XRD patterns (b) and peak shifts (c) of OCDs/I2O4 in EtOH with different concentrations; the FT-IR spectrum of CDs, OCDs/I2O4, and OCDs (d).
TEM and HRTEM images of OCDs/I2O4 (a, b), OCDs/I2O4 in EtOH (c, d), OCDs (e, f).
Schematic of the color change mechanism of OCDs/I2O4.
The UV–Vis spectrum and the color change (inset) of OCDs/I2O4 with different ethanol concentrations.

row.entry: The color change of OCDs/I2O4 in different branded alcoholic beverages. (inset)

head.ce:title: Color change mechanism and application of oxidized carbon dots/I2O4 composite (OCDs/I2O4) in alcoholic beverages

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Light Industry Science and Engineering, Beijing Technology and Business University, Beijing 100048, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Light Industry Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Light Industry Science and Engineering, Beijing Technology and Business University, Beijing 100048, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Currently, ethanol detection in alcoholic beverages typically relies on complicated and costly approaches, including gas chromatography, liquid chromatography, and mass spectrometry, etc. Herein, we developed a non-toxicity, practical, low-cost and portable visual indicator for ethanol detection based on OCDs/I2O4 nanopowders. By color change, the OCDs/I2O4 can visually indicate ethanol concentration ranging from 14 % vol to 56 % vol and the detection limit can be further extended to 2 % vol with a UV-visible spectrum. The OCDs/I2O4 indicator shows good stability and long-term endurance for at least 18 months, and it can be used under different temperatures (at least from 3 °C to 40 °C) and humidity (at least from 10 % to 90 %). A possible mechanism for the color change has been proposed based on the covalent-link hydrogen bonding change between OCDs and I2O4. The proposed OCDs/I2O4 composite offers a convenient and practical solution for ethanol monitoring in various alcoholic beverages.

We developed a non-toxicity, practical, low-cost and portable visual indicator for ethanol detection based on OCDs/I2O4 nanopowders. By color change, the OCDs/I2O4 can visually indicate ethanol concentration ranging from 14 % vol to 56 % vol and the detection limit can be further extended to 2 % vol with a UV–visible spectrum. The OCDs/I2O4 indicator shows good stability and long-term endurance for at least 18 months, and it can be used under different temperatures (at least from 3 °C to 40 °C) and humidity (at least from 10 % to 90 %). A possible mechanism for the color change has been proposed based on the covalent-link hydrogen bonding change between OCDs and I2O4. The proposed OCDs/I2O4 composite offers a convenient and practical solution for ethanol monitoring in various alcoholic beverages.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Alcoholic beverages are popular all over the world, and research has shown that low levels of alcoholic drinks may offer health benefits [1]. However, heavier drinking poses significant risks of irreversible damage to vital organs such as the brain, liver [2], lungs [3], kidneys [4], and nervous system [5]. According to a recent study in Nature Medicine, drinking strong alcohol increases the risks of >200 diseases, including alcoholism, diabetes, gout, stroke, cataracts, and cancer [6].

Currently, ethanol detection in alcoholic beverages typically relies on complicated and costly approaches, including gas chromatography, liquid chromatography, and mass spectrometry etc. [7,8]. These techniques require complex equipment and are also time-consuming, making them impractical for quick, on-the-spot testing, especially in home settings or less affluent areas. Recently, Peng et al. developed smart hydrogel gratings for sensitive and rapid ethanol detection by adjusting the molar ratio of N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAM) and acrylamide (AAm), thereby changing their refractive index and height [9]. Similarly, Hong et al. reported a hydrogel optical fibre sensor for rapid on-site ethanol determination using the rhodamine 6 G was immobilized as a fluorescent indicator [10]. Despite innovations that have been continuously developed, quickly detecting the concentration of ethanol remains a challenge. Herein, we propose a novel method using CDs, which can more direct and faster detection of ethanol.

CDs, quasi-spherical fluorescent particles with sizes ranging from 1 nm to 10 nm, are emerging as a promising alternative for food yield detection due to their non-toxicity, biocompatibility, low cost, chemical inertness, and ease of fabrication [11–13]. The application and applicability of CDs can be tailored by modifying the structures and interfaces, such as incorporating functional groups, which can enhance their chemical and optical properties, expanding their applicability [14–17]. For instance, Latha et al. reported a methanol fluorescent sensor from N-doped oxidized carbon dots (NOCDs) for methanol detection in alcoholic beverages [18]. Yuan et al. reported lychee-like SnO2 hollow microspheres sensitized by carbon quantum dots (CQDs) for ethanol detection, and a multi-fold improvement in gas sensor performance is achieved through the unique electron transport mechanism of carbon quantum dots [19]. Inspired by that work, we aimed to develop a practical and functional ethanol detector. In particular, Koshy et al. reported a low-cost visual indicator based on starch/carbon nanodots incorporating anthocyanin for monitoring the spoilage of pork which can respond to food spoilage is a novel and green development in the packaging field [20]. In this method, visual colour changes were found to shift from purple to green as storage time increased, which has greatly inspired us to modify CDs and make them produce visual colour changes and practical functional values. Our work has developed the OCDs/I2O4, which can quantify concentrations of EtOH on-site with naked eyes with low interferences, significantly improving its convenience and practicability.

In this study, we present a low-cost visual indicator based on OCDs/I2O4 composite, prepared hydrothermally using citric acid as a carbon precursor and NaIO4 as an oxidizing agent. A visual color change from pale yellow to reddish brown within three seconds along the ethanol concentration increases has been achieved. This color variation is attributed to the covalent-link hydrogen bonding change between OCDs and I2O4. The proposed OCDs/I2O4 composite can be used as a low-cost visual indicator for monitoring ethanol in alcoholic beverages, offering a convenient and practical solution. This study may also shed some light on developing economically viable portable sensors to quantify ethanol content in alcoholic beverages. Such a system could be utilized by beverage companies, liquor stores, and even individuals worldwide, making ethanol detection more accessible and practical.

Monohydrated citric acid and ethylenediamine were of grade p.a. and purchased from the Sinopill Beijing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Acetone, ethanol, acetic acid, and sodium periodate were also of grade p.a. and purchased from Beijing Mreda Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). 14 branded liquors (Table 1 ) were purchased from the Yonghui Superstores as common commercial alcohol beverages. All the chemicals were used without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs, OCDs/I2O4, and OCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Preparation of CDs. CDs were synthesized by heating citric acid and ethylenediamine in autoclaves via a "one-pot" preparation method. Samples were prepared immediately to minimize surface contamination during exposure to ambient air and dried under vacuum before use. All solvents were prepared using distilled water. The steps of each synthesis method are based on our previous report and are available in detail in the previous report [21].

Synthesis of OCDs/I2O4. The hydrothermal method was used to prepare OCDs/I2O4. CDs (1 g) and NaIO4 (2 g) were dissolved in 120 mL distilled water, 240 mL 2 % CH3COOH, and 120 mL EtOH, and then stirred for 1 h before hydrothermal heating at 120 °C for 14 h into an autoclave. After the reaction was completed, it was cooled to room temperature, centrifuged and washed. The composite was vacuum-dried at 40 °C for 6 h The preparation process of OCDs/I2O4 is illustrated in Fig. 1 .
Synthesis of OCDs. OCDs/I2O4 (0.1 g) were dissolved in 20 mL ethanol and then stirred for 30 s, centrifuged and washed. The black dawn OCDs were vacuum-dried at 40 °C for 6 h

Measurements and Characterization. The morphology of the samples was observed using a FEI Tecnai G2F30 TEM (USA) with an accelerating voltage of 300 kV. FT-IR spectra were recorded using a Nicolet iS10 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, USA) in the transmission mode over a spectral range of 4000–700 cm−1 with a resolution of 2 cm−1. XRD analysis was performed on an AXS-D2 diffractometer (Bruker, Karlsruhe, Germany) equipped with high-intensity monochromatic Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15406 nm), with a scanning speed fixed at 0.07 °/s and a scanning range of 2θ = 5∼80°

For mimicking baijiu beverages, we configure the different concentrations of ethanol. A certain amount of OCDs/I2O4 (0.05 mg) was added to a 2 mL cuvette, and the remainder of the cuvette was filled with an alcohol mixture ranging from 0 % to 100 % ethanol concentration while keeping the total alcohol content constant. Then we photographed them to record their colour change for comparison.

For branded beverage tests, 2 mL of 14 branded liquors in Table 1 were tested with 0.05 mg of as-prepared OCDs/I2O4, then photographed to record their color change for comparison. The color change of OCDs/I2O4 in various liquors has been illustrated in the inset of Table 1.

For evaluating the detection limits of OCDs/I2O4 for ethanol detection, a series of aqueous ethanol solutions (2 mL) were tested with 0.05 mg OCDs/I2O4. The ultraviolet-visible near-infrared (UV–Vis) spectrum was measured with a SolidSpec-3700i (Smeo Analytical, Shanghai, China) UV–Vis spectroscopy.

CDs is a dark brown powder, which can be homogeneously dispersed in the solvent, resulting in a dark brown solution (Fig. 1a). After functionalization, OCDs/I2O4 is a yellowish powder (Fig. 1b). The fabrication process is illustrated in Fig 1. Both synthesized CDs and OCDs/I2O4 are fine powders, with significant differences in appearance. OCDs/I2O4 exhibited varying appearance and colors when exposed to different ethanol concentrations, enabling visual ethanol detection (Fig. 1c, d).

Structure analysis of CDs, OCDs/I2O4 and OCDs. As shown in the XRD spectra in Fig. 2a , the OCDs/I2O4 composites show distinct I2O4 (#82–2223) peaks at 23.8°, 26.4°, 28.8°, 35.1°, 45.5°, 46.5°, 47.5°, 52.3°, 55.3° and 57.1° corresponding to diffraction planes (202), (111), (012), (102), (323), (202), (032), (332), (014) and (330), respectively. OCDs peak at 19.1° and 40.5°, demonstrating the OCDs/I2O4 composite was successfully fabricated. The peak of OCDs shifts from 32.7° to 30.7°, suggesting the progressive formation of chemically cross-linked domains between OCDs and I2O4 [22]. In Fig. 2b, the OCDs/I2O4 composites in different percentages of EtOH have been compared and the intensity of the (012) declined as the ethanol concentration increased from 0 to 80 wt% and then disappeared thoroughly at 100 wt%. This is due primarily to the diffusion of ethanol molecules in OCDs/I2O4, which destroyed the hydrogen bond and affected the cross-linking of OCDs/I2O4 [22]. Additionally, as shown in Fig. 2c, a right shift of (012) has been observed along with the increase of the EtOH concentration, which also agrees with the broken hydrogen bonds in the previous report [22].

FT-IR spectrum was employed to analyse the chemical bonding and composition (Fig. 2b). The spectrum of CDs was similar to the previous report [23], peaks at 1546 cm−1, 1394 cm−1, 1035 cm−1 are attributed to stretching vibrations of NH, CO and CN, respectively. Amide I and II spectral bands were observed at ∼1696 cm−1 and 1546 cm−1 attributed to the vibration of C = O and NH, indicating the presence of the -NHCO- linker, consistent with prior reports [24,25]. The peaks of OCDs/I2O4 at 3420 cm−1, 1670 cm−1, 1410 cm−1 and 1050 cm−1 are attributed to stretching vibrations of OH, C = O, CO and CN, respectively. The peaks of OCDs at 2900 cm−1, 1696 cm−1, 1394 cm−1, and 1035 cm−1 are attributed to stretching vibrations of CH, C = O, CO and CN, respectively. In OCDs/I2O4, the peak at 1650 cm−1 is assigned as the stretching of C = O. According to a previous report [27], intra-molecular hydrogen bonding lowers the carbonyl stretching frequency by about 50 cm−1. The carbonyl group frequency also indicates that the OCDs/I2O4 have hydrogen bonds because the general carbonyl group frequency is ∼1696 cm−1 (without any hydrogen bonding), as shown in the peak of CDs and OCDs. The red shifting of this peak is due to the hydrogen bonding, indicating the formation of intra- and interchain hydrogen bonds between the OCDs and I2O4 [26]. Furthermore, the spectra of OCDs/I2O4 exhibited a characteristic peak at 3420 cm−1 corresponding to the OH stretching. In the spectra of OCDs, the intensity of this absorption peak decreased, indicating the formation of cross-linked OCDs/I2O4 composite from hydroxyl groups, which also agreed with the previous report [27–29].

Morphology of OCDs/I2O4, OCDs/I2O4 in EtOH and OCDs. TEM images were captured to examine the morphologies of as-fabricated OCDs/I2O4, OCDs/I2O4 in EtOH and OCDs in EtOH. Fig. 3a and 3b present the HRTEM images of OCDs/I2O4. The average size of the dispersed OCD is ∼5 nm and OCDs/I2O4 exhibit a cross-linking structure (Fig. 3a). The HRTEM in Fig. 3b exhibits the lattice fringes of d ≈ 0.323 nm and 0.326 nm that corresponds to the (012) and (140) crystallographic place of I2O4. Fig. 3c and 3d present the TEM images of OCDs/I2O4 in EtOH. Fig. 3c demonstrates that the crystal I2O4 nanostructure with a length of 100–200 nm. The OCD nanoparticles were well-dispersed on the I2O4 with a size range of 5–10 nm. The HRTEM image in Fig. 3d exhibits the lattice fringes of d ≈ 0.350 nm and 0.351 nm, corresponding to the (302) and (210) crystallographic place of I2O4. It turns out that in the presence of 2 mL ethanol, OCDs gradually peel off. Fig. 3e demonstrates that the OCDs exhibit cross-linking phenomena. The HRTEM image in Fig. 3f exhibits that I2O4 was dissolved by ethanol, resulting in there is no lattice.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Color change mechanism of OCDs/I2O4 in EtOH
ce:section.ce:para: A possible color change mechanism of OCDs/I2O4 in alcoholic beverages has been proposed based on XRD and TEM and illustrated in Fig. 4 . The color change of OCDs/I2O4 in EtOH is due to the hydroxyl group of ethanol stripping the OCDs from I2O4 and turning it into a dark sediment by destroying the OCDs/I2O4 composite. It is clear that the higher concentration of EtOH contains more hydroxyl groups and provides more hydrogen bonds, resulting in more OCDs (dark brown) peeled off OCDs/I2O4.

As shown in Fig. 4a, after exposure to EtOH, OCDs/I2O4 decompose into a dark precipitate of OCDs in EtOH and a yellow supernatant. Then, dropping 1 mL supernatant into starch, the starch turned blue after ethanol was volatilized. The reaction between iodine and starch resulted in a blue color change, as observed in our study. The observation of branched-chain starch turning blue when exposed to Iodine confirms the presence of Iodine in the supernatant generated upon the addition of ethanol. Iodine was detected in the supernatant, which caused ethanol to promote the conversion of I2O4 into Iodine. Furthermore, the addition of ethanol induces a transformation of OCDs/I2O4 into OCDs and I2. This transformation is accompanied by structural changes and cross-linking in OCDs/I2O4, which ultimately leads to a darker color with increasing ethanol concentration.

As shown in Fig. 4b, when adding 20 % acetic acid, the OCDs/I2O4 have become shallow gradually. The addition of 20 % acetic acid has been observed to promote the conversion of IO3 − and I − to I2, which results in the OCD powder becoming lighter yellow than before. This results in a lighter colour due to the bleaching effect of I2. This result can expand the scope of discolouration, exaggerating its practical value.

As the concentration of acetic acid increases, a significant number of H+ are surrounded, which will disrupt the covalent hydrogen bond between OCDs/I2O4 composite. This process ultimately leads to the dissolution of the composite in acetic acid with a concentration of greater than 50 %. When adding 50 % acetic acid above, the OCDs/I2O4 dissolved in acetic acid gradually, as shown in Fig. 4c. After adding 50 % acetic acid above, OCDs/I2O4 were decomposed by H+ and turned into yellow liquid. It is clear that the higher the concentration of CH3COOH contains the more H+, which are decomposed OCDs/I2O4 composite. This phenomenon proves that the bind of OCDs/I2O4 by hydrogen bonding between OCDs and I2O4.

Branded alcoholic beverage tests. As shown in Table 1, OCDs/I2O4 can successfully show different concentrations of EtOH in the liquors by a color change in the detection of various branded alcoholic beverages. Detection limit tests. As illustrated in Fig. 5 , the color change of OCDs/I2O4 can be visually observed until 14 % vol (the minimum visual limit), while the UV–Vis spectrum may extend the detection limit to 2 % vol.

To exam the practical applications of OCDs/I2O4 under different environmental conditions, the EtOH detection has been done with various temperatures and humidity, illustrated in Table 2 and ESI Fig. S1. Very minimum affection from temperature and humidity has been observed, showing high practical ability of OCDs/I2O4 for EtOH detection. To evaluate the specificity of OCDs/I2O4 for ethanol detection, we tested the common food additives, including fructose, glucose, and glycerol, and common organic solvents, including methanol, ether acetate, and ethyl acetate. No obvious color change has been observed in the above solutions, indicating that OCDs/I2O4 exhibits strong specificity to EtOH from other common food additives and organic solvents, highlighting its specificity for ethanol detection, detailed in Table 3 and ESI Fig. S2.

To evaluate the stability of OCDs/I2O4 for ethanol detection, we tested the sample synthesized on June 25, 2023 (over 18 months) and stored it in glass bottles at room temperature. As illustrated in ESI Fig. S3, the OCDs/I2O4 can be successfully applied in EtOH detection without obvious changes in properties, showing good durability and long-term stability of OCDs/I2O4 for practical applications.

In this work, we have designed and synthesized OCDs/I2O4 through a simple "one-pot" preparation method in autoclaves. XRD and TEM showed that the lattice spacing of 0.323 nm and 0.326 nm corresponds to the (012) and (140) planes of crystalloid OCDs/I2O4, respectively. OCDs/I2O4 exhibit cross-linking phenomena, indicating CDs and I2O4 form the new material by hydrogen bond. This work provided a feasible strategy to improve the functional groups on the CD surface, indicating the interaction between OCDs and I2O4. Our proposed OCDs/I2O4 composite can be used as a low-cost visual indicator for monitoring ethanol in alcoholic beverages, offering a convenient and practical solution and responding to a novel and green development in the food field. In the long term, this study opens the possibility of developing an economically viable portable sensor system to quantify ethanol content in alcoholic beverages. Such a system could be utilized by beverage companies, liquor stores, and even individuals worldwide, making ethanol detection more accessible and practical.

Ming-yu Shi: Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Visualization, Formal analysis, Software, Conceptualization, Data curation, Resources, Writing – original draft. Ao Li: Resources, Conceptualization, Investigation. Tian-hao Ji: Project administration, Validation, Resources. Da-jian Jv: Funding acquisition. Zhe-mi Xu: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Funding: This research was supported by the funding of the School of Light Industry Science and Engineering in Beijing Technology and Business University. We thank Shule Zhang for the detailed experiment suggestions and valuable discussion.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.cartre.2024.100440.
